FArR PouncAL PRACTICES CoMMissroN 
428 J Street • Suire 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
Samantha A. Arens 
Staff Counsel 
Chief Counsel's Office 
California Energy Commi.1,sion 
1516 Ninth Street 
Sacramento, CA 95814-5512 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
July 30, 2014 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-14-125 
Dear Ms. Arens: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provi.1,ions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that the Commission is not a finder of fact when 
rendering advice (111 re Og/es/Jy ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ). Moreover, because you request 
general guidance and your request does not involve a particular decision, we render informal 
• 
'l 
asĩ1stance.-
QUESTION AND ANALYSIS 
This letter responds to your general inquiry about the Act's exceptions for gifts of travel. 
As an initial matter, you are correct that the two provisions that you discuss are different in 
application and effect.:' 
• 
Section 89506 is an exception to the gift limits of the Act. Despite the application of Section 
89506, the payment is still considered a gift to the official for purposes of reporting and 
disqualification. 
1 The Polt1ical Reform Act is contaim:d in Government Code Section 81000 through 9 IO 1-t All srntutory 
rclcrcnccs arc 10 the Gmernmcnl Code. unless olhcrn ie indicated. The regulations or the Fair Political Practices 
Commi.<..sion arc contained in Section 18110 through 18997 of Tille 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory refcrem.:es arc 10 Tnlc 2. Di\ ision 6 or the California Code uf Regulations. unlcs othcrn ise indicated. 
1 Informal assislance docs not provide the requestor 1\ith the 1mmun11y provided hy an opinion or formal 
\\ntten ad\ice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
·
1 A third po-;ihle exception allm\ s alternale disclosure ( Form 80 I ) where a pay men I is more a gift to the 
agency tlian a gift 10 the specific official. You have not asked ahoul this provision. 


File No. 1-14-125 
Page No. 2 
• Regulation 18950(c)(2), on the other hand, was intended to carve out a more limited 
exception by which a payment would not be considered a gift or income under limited 
circumstances. In essence, that exception recognizes that in some circumstances money is 
exchanged between agencies so that an official may perform his or her job. These 
transactions were never intended to be reportable or potentially disqualifying gifts or income 
to the official because the payments were used to conduct agency business and not to provide 
a personal benefit to the official. 
Additionally, the two exceptions are not mutually exclusive and, under some occasions, 
either exception may be available. However, either one or the other must be chosen, and an 
official cannot cross apply the benefits from one to another. For example, Section 89506 
specifically allows any travel related expenses and up to three days of unrestricted lodging for a 
limited speaking engagement on the middle day. Under Regulation 18950 an overnight stay of 
even one night would require some justification, the travel amounts would, for the most part, be 
limited to applicable state per diem rates, and there would be other restrictions on the cost of the 
travel. 
You posited the following specific examples: 
( 1) An Energy Commission program manager.flies to /nine to .\peak at a symposium 
hosted by the UlliversNy of Califomia at Irvine ( UC/). The program 11umager speaks about 
hydrogen refueling station development in Califomia, i11c/11dillg program.\' nm by the Energy 
Commission to implemellt .'itate policy 011 hydrogen infrastrucfllre. UC/ has receiw!d hydrogen­
related grants from the Energy Commi.\·.\·ion in the past. UC/ offers to reimburse the Energy 
Commission employee's travel, lodging, and per diem expenses 011 the day immediately 
preceding the pre.rn11tatio11, the day of tlze prese11tatio11, and the day after tire presentation. The 
ammmt of these paymellls exceeds $440. 
Regulation 18950(c) states that there is an exception for a payment for travel and related 
per diem expenses that is received from a state, local, or federal government agency (UCI) for 
education, training, or other interagency programs or purposes (the manager in question is 
providing training and education at the UCI symposium as part of that mwwger 's job at the 
CEC).4 Therefore, the payment appears to qualify for the exception in Regulation 18950. l with 
respect to the "related per diem expenses." Because your hypothetical did not provide any 
information explaining why a three day stay is necessary to make a speech at UCI, it appears that 
a portion of this travel payment would be in excess of the amount allowable under Regulation 
18950(c) and would not qualify for the exception. 
However, if the official wishes to receive a three day stay in return for the presentation, 
he or she may do so by reporting the gift under Section 89506. The Commission does not 
O lndicia that this is a work related task include CEC treating the manager's time at the symposium a!> 
compensated work time and the manager might accrue informal CTO for the hours at the symposium. At the other 
extreme might he a situation where an employee might want lo discuss maners at the symposium on his own time 
outside of CEC control. Obviously these arc two extremes and circumstances in the middles will need to be 
evaluated independently. 


File No.1-14-125 
Page No. 3 
regulate how an official uses agency time, so it would be up to the agency to determine what 
provisions of state law would allow the extra two days' time away from the job. 
(2) A project manager at the Energy Commission flies to Long Beach to speak at a 
workshop, hosted by the U.S. E11viro11111ellfal Protection Agency, 011 emission reduction 
strategies. The project manager gfres a keynote address 011 climate clumge science and the 
Energy Commission's research 011 climate change impacts 011 the energy sector, and in 
particular 011 a higlzly-pote11t gree11ho11se gas used in the electric industry. The federal agency 
pays the Energy Commission employee's travel, lodging, mu/ per diem expenses 011 the day 
immediately preceding the presentation, the day of the presellfatio11, a11d the day after the 
presellfation. The (llllOWll of these payments exceeds $440. 
Under these hypothetical facts, the payment again appears to qualify for the provisions of 
Regulation 18950 with repect to the "related per diem expenses." Because your hypothetical 
did not provide any information explaining why a three day stay is necessary to make a speech in 
Long Beach, it appears that a portion of this travel payment would be in excess of the amount 
allowable and would not qualify for the exception 
However, if the official wishes to receive a three.day stay in return for the presentation, 
he she may do so by reporting the gift under Section 89506. The Commission does not regulate 
how an official uses agency time, so it would be up to the agency to determine what provisions 
of stale law would allow to the extra two days' time away from the job 
(3) A11 Energy Commi.\'.1·ion analyst flies to Washington, D. C. for the pw7mse of gi\•ing a 
prese11tatio11 at a public meeting hosted by the U.S. Departmelll of Energy. The analyst speaks 
0,1 tire Energy Com111issio11 's role in setting solar electric eq11ipme11t requiremems for 11111/tiple 
statewide solar programs. /11 particular, tire E11ergy Commi.l'sion maintains a database of 
eligible solar electric equipment. The pw7Jose of the DOE meeting was to find ways in which the 
Energy Commission's equipmellf database can help other states achieve solar goals, and to find 
otherfimding sources for the Energy Commi.\·sion to maintain the database. The National 
Re11ewable Energy Laboratory, a federallyfimded national laboratory, which prepared a 1l'hite 
paper for the Energy Commission 011 maintenance of the database, reimburses the analyst for 
trm·el, lodging, and per diem expenses for the day before, the day of, and the day after her 
preselllation. The amo11nt of these paymellfs exceeds $440. 
Again, under the above example, the official may claim the full cost of the travel 
payments as a gift under Section 89506 because he or she was making a public presentation on a 
matter of public policy within the United States. The gift could include up to three nights at the 
hotel, all meals during the period, and there would be no restrictions on the actual tran!\portation 
expense. The payment would have to be reported as a gift by the official. 
In the alternative, it appears the payment could qualify under the provisions of Regulation 
18950. I as the type of payment made in conjunction with official agency business. Under this 
method, the payment would not be reportable by the official but it would have to meet the 
provisions required by the regulation including reporting by the agency on a Form 80 I. If this 


File No. 1-14-125 
Page No. 4 
method is used, the travel must comply with state travel and per diem rules for out-of-Mate 
travel. 
( 4) A senior engineer at the Energy Com111is.'lio11 1•olunteers Oil a federal advisory 
committee fullded by multiple federal agencies. lmportalltly, tlze ellgilleer volunteers 011 lzis own 
time and 1101 Oil belzalf of the Energy Commission. Tlze federal advisory committee is created by 
the federal govemmellt to produce a national climate assessment for the White House ( a 11011-
bi11di11g recomme11datio11). The e11gi11eer is considered, for purposes of his work 011 the 
committee, to be a llon-salaried employee of the federal government. The e11gi11eer's trm1el costs 
to Washington D.C., mu! lodging alld per diem while there, are reimbursed by tlze Department of 
Commerce. Those costs exceed $440 per trip. 
Here the senior engineer is being paid travel expenses in connection with his or her job as 
a "non-salaried employee of the federal government." As such, there are no reporting 
obligations under the Act. (Sec Section 82030(b)(2).) 
( 5) A Commissio11er at the Energy Co111111is.\·io11 receives mz offer to serve as a member of 
a11 energy-related federal advisory committee. The committee is fimded by the Departmellt of 
Ellergy. The committee makes 11011 binding recomme11datio11s to federal officials regardi11g 
federal e11ergy policy matters. The Commissioner receives 110 salary as a member of tlze 
committee, /mt travel costs to Washillgto11 D. C., alld /odgillg alld per diem while there, are 
reimbursed by the Department of Energy. Those cost.\' exceed $440 per trip. 
Same answer as previous example. 
I hope this general guidance is helpful. lf you have other questions on this matter, or are 
confronted with an actual factual situation, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
neral Counsel 
. Wallace 
nt General Counsel

File No. 1-14-125 Page No. 2 • Regulation 18950(c)(2), on the other hand, was intended to carve out a more limited exception by which a payment would not be considered a gift or income under limited circumstances. In essence, that exception recognizes that in some circumstances money is exchanged between agencies so that an official may perform his or her job. These transactions were never intended to be reportable or potentially disqualifying gifts or income to the official because the payments were used to conduct agency business and not to provide a personal benefit to the official. Additionally, the two exceptions are not mutually exclusive and, under some occasions, either exception may be available. However, either one or the other must be chosen, and an official cannot cross apply the benefits from one to another. For example, Section 89506 specifically allows any travel related expenses and up to three days of unrestricted lodging for a limited speaking engagement on the middle day.
